title: Big Tech Companies Found Responsible for Hurting Kids Online
summary: In two big court cases, juries said that YouTube and Meta (which owns Instagram and Facebook) did something wrong. They made their apps and websites too addictive for children and teens, which can hurt their mental health. The companies knew this could be bad but still designed their platforms to keep kids hooked. In California, they have to pay $6 million, and in New Mexico, Meta must pay $375 million for hiding problems with child safety. A tech expert, Jacob Ward, explained that this is important because it shows the companies' designs, not just what people post, are causing harm. Parents are speaking out, saying they want to protect kids from these addictive features. This is a big step in holding tech companies accountable for how they affect young people.

keywords.explanation: something that makes you want to keep using it a lot, like a game or app
when someone is found responsible for doing something wrong in court
how you feel in your mind, like being happy or sad
money that a company has to pay because they caused harm
a website or app where people can share things, like Instagram or YouTube

questions.question: What did the juries say about YouTube and Meta?
options: They made apps too addictive for kids
They helped kids learn new things
They made apps safer for everyone
questions.correct_answer: They made apps too addictive for kids

questions.question: How much money did Meta have to pay in New Mexico?
What is Meta the parent company of?
Why is the California case a big deal according to Jacob Ward?
options: It blames the design of the platforms
It gives kids more screen time
questions.correct_answer: It blames the design of the platforms

questions.question: What can addictive apps hurt in kids?

background_read: This article talks about court cases where big tech companies like YouTube and Meta were found responsible for making their apps too addictive for children and teens. Addiction means kids can't stop using the apps, which can make them feel sad or anxious. The companies had to pay millions of dollars because they knew this could happen but didn't fix it. This is important because it shows that companies need to think about how their designs affect young people, not just what users post. Parents and experts are happy because it might lead to safer apps for kids in the future.
Article_Structure: WHO: YouTube, Meta (owner of Instagram and Facebook), juries, parents, and Jacob Ward (a tech writer).
WHAT: Juries found the companies liable for designing addictive platforms that harm kids' mental health, and they must pay damages.
WHERE: In California and New Mexico courts.
WHY: To hold tech companies accountable for hurting children and to set a precedent for safer designs.

perspectives.description: The courts are using new laws to say companies are responsible for how their designs affect kids.
Parents are worried and speaking out to protect their children from addictive apps.
